source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Each one of us has a certain view of the world.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch

text: We have an a kind of a...

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: a verse of what life is.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And our view of the world, both the lights and horror files.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Welcome to Being In The Way, the Alan Watts podcast.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch

speaker: Ram Dass
text: and remastered for this series.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Today's podcast was co-produced with the

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Romdas P. here now podcast network.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And our music is by Zakir who's saying courtesy of moment records.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And now here's Alan Watts and the limits of language.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Unfortunately, when we understand something,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: most of us really mean that we've got it translated into words.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Although we understand an enormous number of things that we don't know about in words at all.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We understand how to breathe because we do it.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But we're not able to put it into words.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But somehow we've got ourselves into the frame of mind

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: where unless we can put something into words,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: especially the kind of things I've been discussing,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: we have the feeling that we don't understand it.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And all of us here are people who are really intellectuals.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We are a literate crowd of people.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We read books and are very much influenced by the literary aspect of our culture.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: In our education, we have cultivated three kinds of intelligence.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: principally verbal intelligence,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: many monic intelligence, which is memory, and computational intelligence.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Rather incidentally, in our hand, we may, if we did some kind of physical education,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: have cultivated kinesthetic intelligence.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But in addition to those that would be social intelligence,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: aesthetic intelligence, and probably some others.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And these are not developed.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: A professor at Harvard, when I was there, said, once that nothing is intellectually respectable,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: unless it can be put into words.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And he was commenting on Timothy Leris investigations into states of consciousness induced

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: by psychedelic chemicals.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And I thought, you know, if nothing is intellectually respectable,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: unless it can be put into words, a last, a last, for the Department of Music,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: for the Department of Finance, for the Department of Physical Education.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And one of the great problems now, the going on in the academic world,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: is, for example, that just to take an illustration, traditional academics,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: cannot understand what black studies are about.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: They think that there is no culture here.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It's something, how could you possibly do that?

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It's like an old film where it was called a Yankee at Oxford,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: played by Robert Taylor.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And he got a Rhodes scholarship to Oxford.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And it shows his first interview with his tutor.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And he says he's going to specialize in American history.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And the tutor says, well, I don't think that will give you very much to occupy our time.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Oh, funny.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But you see, you can study anything and depending on your intelligence,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: you can make a whole world out of it, because you will always find in anything you look at

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: an infinitude of subtlety.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So I've maintained the theory that every living being, whatever, whether it be a cat, dog,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: a giraffe or a microorganism, thinks that it's human, with full justification.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Because it feels just as you and I do, that it's the center of the universe.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And there are all kinds of complicated things going on.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And it has the same sensation of being intelligent and of possessing a high culture that you and I possess.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And I believe this to be true of the very vegetables that they all are highly sensitive and intelligent.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And that if we could learn somehow or other to speak their language, we would discover that their culture is quite as complex as ours.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And this is one of the great things that are, well, it's a opens and blows once mind.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: To realize how the world would look from the point of view of another kind of consciousness altogether.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And so that's why it's enormously important that we do have such things as black studies.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Because they challenge us with the possibility of putting in the academic world a point of view entirely different from our own.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So I've advocated for years that high schools teach Chinese, not because simply that we've got to relate somehow to the Chinese people and not to be able to understand their language.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But because the Chinese culture was an extremely complex and highly evolved culture with a language completely different from our own.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And therefore if you study that language, you begin to see that there are ways of thinking based on principles based on a kind of logic that is not one's own common sense.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And therefore by studying this very different thing, you get a perspective on what your own assumptions are.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: See, most of us don't know what our assumptions are.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We operate on principles which we haven't really examined.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And by studying a completely different culture, you begin to understand what your principles are.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And therefore it's tremendously important that we study the African world, as well as the Far Eastern, and the American Indian world,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: which has Gary Snyder has said is almost further from us than the world of the Chinese.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: That's why emphasized yesterday, Carlos Castaneda's books, where you read about Don Juan and Don Juan's fantastic laughter at this good academic anthropologists attitude to life.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Now he says series, and he's trying to get everything categorized in a nice academic way so that he can write a PhD thesis on the American Indians use of mind transforming plants.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It reminds me of a story which a great professor has Stanford told against himself, Frederick Spiegelberg, who's been a friend of mine for many years.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: He got a grant from the Rockefeller Foundation to go to Asia, specifically India, to make a study of the present state and health of Indian spirituality.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: In other words, are the spiritual traditions of Asia well and truly alive?

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So he went and made all these studies, and he got hooked on Shriyara Bindu.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But when he was on his way back, he went through Hong Kong.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And he asked some friends there, whether he could meet a Taoist sage, is the other and he's such people still alive.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: They said, we yes, there is a very famous Taoist sage, and he lives a little way from Hong Kong on one of the islands.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So they took a boat and went to this island.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Just a little rocky island and they had to anchor a small harbor and climb up a mountain path until they came to a straw hut where this man lived.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And this sage was exceedingly fat in norms like hotel, you know, the fat Buddha, two-tie.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And so the interpreter said, this is Professor Spikleberg from Stanford University, who is coming here to the east on a ground from the Rockefeller Foundation to enquire about the vitality of Asian spirituality.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And one of the places you could tell him something as to whether the Taoist tradition is still alive.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And this man began very gently to chuckle and it grew louder and louder and louder.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And all his flesh vibrated with laughter.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Laugh, laugh, laugh, laugh, laugh, laugh and it got drunk.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Other complete bellow. And that was all. That was the end of the interview.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So there are things going on, in other words, there are whole ways of life that cannot be contained within the networks that we regard as sensible or academically respectable.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And this includes, of course, naturally, the ways of life of the plants.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We say of somebody who is hopelessly sick, terminal cancer case, that they've become a mere vegetable.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But that's a very insulting thing to say about vegetables. No vegetables are mere vegetable.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And the more you know about botané and the more even beyond that, you use your imagination and try to put yourself into the situation of a rose.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: The more you begin to penetrate your skull, that this is a very important form of life.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Emerson saw this in the famous passage where he said, these roses under my window.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Do not worry about whether they are better than former roses, or whether that succeeding roses will be better than they.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: There is simply the rose. It exists with God today.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But human beings, heedless of the riches which surround them, are always on tiptoe to foresee a future.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And they don't know how to live completely here and now.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And that is the main thing that we don't see about certain other cultures and certain other species altogether.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: A lot of things that the African cultures have contributed to us, especially in the world of music, all have to do with being alive now as distinct from making plans for some other time.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And what we describe as, when we talk about various cultures, whether they be Australian or oceanic, South Sea or African or whatever we say, these people are primitives.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And what we really mean by that is they are not following our culture plan of working for the future.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: There are people who have arrived and therefore are happy to be where they are. But we have come around bumbling and interfering with their wildlife and telling them that because they are not progressive.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: They are not really cultivated. And all such people that are called primitive live in a non-historical world.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It has been said, happy are the people who have no history. Because what is history consists of. History consists of a record of power games, of conquests, of battles, of disturbances, of people on the make.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But where one has no history, you have culture. You have attention to full going with the regularly repeated ordinary things that go on from day to day.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And the things that go on from day to day are, of course, cooking, cultivating plants, hunting, making love.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And once full attention goes into these things. And because one's full attention goes into these things, they cultivated into very beautiful arts.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And this way of non-historical life is what we all in the West, very much need to know. Do you realize how our consciousness is affected by our being constantly exposed to what is called the news?

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We read newspapers, we hear news on the radio, and we are given the impression that the world is a kind of career.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: There is this thing going on, which is history. And the newspapers tell us all about it, we get frantically excited about it.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We hear of horrors in justices, games going on, political games, campaigns, or terrifying news.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And our glance respond to it, they secreted lemon, and get us all ready to fight wickedness and injustice.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And it's absolutely nothing you can do about it. Only very influential people can read the newspaper and something ghastly has gone on and pick up the telephone.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And call a friend and say, what the hell are you doing? And make some difference to it. Call a senator, call the president, call a justice of the Supreme Court. The ordinary person will never even get through, but will still have all this adrenal fury.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Going on inside, which completely frustrates you, because there's nothing you can do. There's no way of disposing of this chemical.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So we are in a non-functioning, communicational nervous system. Now, just to say, all the radio, television, newspapers, and so on, all the information everybody is being given is really useless.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Because nothing do about it. So that the electrical communication system in which we're living is to a very large extent, not really functioning as a communication system in the same way that our own nervous system does function as a communication system.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: For example, if you've ever been put in the embarrassing position of being a celebrity, you go on television, you go on radio, you write a book.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And the radio, the television, the book gives the impression to the recipient of the message that you're addressing that person directly.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And they feel that they've had a personal communication from you and they respond. They want to ask the question.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Maybe they want a complete psychoanalysis. They write you letters and you get an enormous bag of mail.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: As absolutely nothing you can do about it. If you answer your mail, you wouldn't be able to do any of your everyday work.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So what do you do? You either throw it in the waste basket or you have a form letter, which says explains what this situation is.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Thank you very much. I'm so glad you liked what I said. But I'll, I'll last, it is logistically impossible to reply.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And these people then get very offended. And they think, well, you must be high and mighty. And so important that you don't dare to speak to the ordinary man.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But what an earth is one to do? For example, I get asked by all kinds of interesting organizations.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: They may be struggling unitary in churches or something of that kind. Would I please come and talk to their people?

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: I've got all kinds of high school students who would very, very interested, read my books and would like a talk, but last we don't have any money.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And we could pay you maybe your cab failed, come over. And I'm embarrassed by having to say, I'm sure that the work you're doing is extremely interesting and that I would find it interesting.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But how am I to choose if I give my services free to you? Everybody else will say, wouldn't why did you choose them rather than us?

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And so, I have to charge you money and say, if you really want me, you will have to pay so much. And you must trust me as to what I'm going to do with it. I may give it away to somebody else. But that's up to me.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But if you really want and you mean it, that you sincere about wanting these services, you will have to pay for it.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Otherwise you will have to give me something that represents your own energy, no one is money. Now I will see that then one gets being accused of being commercial.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So I'm saying this as an example of the situation in which we live, whereby communication doesn't communicate.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We started out yesterday, where I was trying to make the basic point, that there is a vast, vast difference between the world which is and the world which is described.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: The world of the television, the newspaper, the movie, the novel, the book, the review, a time magazine, life magazine, newsweek, etc etc etc etc.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We take to be, if we have been acclimated to the literary culture of a Western industrial state, we take all this information to be what is actually happening.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Whereas it is nothing of the kind. Just as your opinion in yourself is not yourself. The news is not what is happening. It is not what is going on.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It's a particularly slanted view. It is the expression of the limited intellects of politicians and reporters. What is actually going on in the world is far, far different.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: I think everybody should read how men has a book, Stepan Wolf. Because in that book he gives you an insight into a culture which most of us despise.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: That is the culture of what we would call, what is the word, vapid people who play in jazz bands and who frequent certain kinds of restaurants and bars and have interest in clothes that are flashy.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We would say such people are quite superficial. But he shows you how there is a depth to that culture, in an extremely interesting way.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And when we must realize that how Manheza is also the author of the last balanced field, the book about the most incredibly sophisticated culture of the future.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So what I am saying is therefore that every view that we take of the world, every selection we make of what it is that is important to notice is simply one way of looking at things and that there are an infinity of other ways of looking. This is what great painters show us.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: He painters teachers to see many years ago, painters were primarily concerned with portrait painting. They painted human faces, human figures.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Well, they felt that human figures gradually dawned on them were not always to be looked out against white walls. So they began to put landscape in the background mountains and forests and distant cities.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But at the time they were doing that, that background was regarded as what we now call in electronics, noise, static, that which you disregard.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But after a while, some of these painters began to be completely fascinated by the landscape in the background.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And they said to the human figure, hey, get out of the way, move over, I want to look at this other thing. And we began to get landscape painting. And when we first had landscape painting, ordinary jose looked at it and said, that's not what I call a picture.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And it was regarded as very avant-garde art, landscape painting. What's significant about that? But after a while people got used to it and began to see because the artists had shown them that the landscape is beautiful.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And people began to open their eyes and look at nature. And before they didn't do that, you must be amazed if you could put yourself in the state of mind of a cleric or scholar living in about 1300 and traveling through the Alps.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Well, you would think going through the Alps that the mountains are absolutely gorgeous. But this scholar only saw them as a threat. And perfect nuisance that he had to climb through, he didn't see anything beautiful about them until artists had shown them.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Then now we go to the state park at your cemetery, Jackson Hole or Yellowstone or wherever. And there's always a place called inspiration point where you go up a road to an outlook place and all the tourists from Iowa come and stand there and say, oh, it's just like a picture.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Yeah, yeah, these postcards of sunsets and all the kind of thing and everybody says it's real pretty because the artists have shown them. Well, in the course of time other artists began to do different things.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: People like Jackson Pollock experimented with drooling paint on the things because they thought, why does the artist always have to represent something, which happens in nature in a natural way, couldn't we originate a happening in the same way as nature does it.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So after all these modern painters went to work and increasingly have begun to convince people to look at their works and notice them, we were now be able to go down a Chicago street where there's an old building and there's a brick wall with bird droppings on it and slightly torn off posters that were originally attached to their scratches.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Mr. Stan look at that and say, oh, it's just like a picture.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Now then we move on from there landscape was revealed to us by the painters and later on soundscape is revealed to us by the musicians.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It's a certain stage of its development, what is music? Music is a very formal statement, a very highly ordered articulate sequence of sounds and originally of course organized around words to song to say something in a summerous way.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Then later the song is accompanied with instruments. But then people began to notice that when you listen to the instruments there is an enormous amount of background noise.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: As a consider for example, the merely mechanical noise of a symphony orchestra, which we exclude in a certain time and still today when you record in a radio station.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: They try to have soundproof rooms so that no noise that is not relevant can be heard.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Although you sitting in front of your own radio and listening to the broadcast which originated in this room here traffic and airplanes and all kinds of jazz going on outside, they must not have that going on in that room.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So we get what we call a very high-five recording processes so that all that will be excluded and this involves an enormous hassle.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It's very expensive to construct all this equipment.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: When you realize that what's going to be the end product finally is nothing like what you broadcast because of all the incidental noise in the listeners home.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So you make television and you put up these sets where you are following an ancient dramatic tradition that you're simulating life.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But nobody must see the props as it were.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: You know, behind any scene, there's wires and tape and all kinds of things that hold it together like a scaffolding.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So there's a front in the Chinese tradition of the theatre.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: The prop man walks onto the stage, but he was black, which means that he's technically invisible.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And the Japanese puppet theatre called Bunrakul, the people who work the puppets were black. They're technically invisible.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But there they are. But in television, especially educational television, which is one of the funniest things going.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Everybody has to sit at a desk with a bookshelves behind them to look as if they're in a study or a classroom, never blackboard.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And no camera is visible. Some of the best television you can do is television in the round, where you have people discussing something, you know, in a group, in cameras in every direction.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Well, one camera will show another camera. But it doesn't matter. We don't television and everybody knows it.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Why pretend that you're in the theatre? Or that there isn't any camera present? And what you're looking at.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: In an obviously artificial situation, something behind a flat, impenetrable screen, why pretend that it's real life?

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: That's as convention. And so why I'm talking about this is that the considering such things makes you aware of how much your knowledge of the world is

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: is conventional knowledge. A selection of particular things to which you attend, to which you have been brainwashed to notice, and the rest is disregarded.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So that it would be, as I've suggested, that the world is like a raw-shark plot, and there is an official interpretation of the plot, which we call the culture.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And everybody agrees, of course, that's the way it is. Until some great artists, some great genius, begin showing us that you can look at the world in an entirely different way.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Everybody says, this is crazy. Oh, here. Things aren't like that. But if he insists and goes on long enough, we can now look back at, say, Zanz paintings and say, yes, it does look like that.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We can look at Van Gogh and say, oh yes, he really did see how fields are. He's taught us to see. When I was a child, I was exposed very early in life to Chinese art.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And at first I thought, it's fantastic. And I think some took like that. Mountains don't look like that. Flowers don't look like that. They're all stylized. Weird.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: But when I got used to Chinese art, I suddenly saw, they were looking at things with incredible accuracy.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: I once went to the Victoria and Albert Museum in London. I was very interested in Japanese woodblock prints. And there was a print of a tiger. And at first I thought this tiger had never be unraveled.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It is the most complicated, fantastic tiger I ever saw in my life. But I was interested in it just because it was so weird. So I got out my drawing block and I copied the print.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And as I followed very carefully, all the limbs of the tiger, I discovered that their arrangement was completely logical. The thing was not at all weird. It was simply a very, very clever straight picture of a tiger beautifully done.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And so finally, when I went to the far east, we immediately had a joke, which was, isn't it strange? How much Japan looks like Japan?

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And I mean, here were all these paintings, standing out right in front of you in everyday life.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Whereas we formally thought they were weird. So the moral is, each one of us has a certain view of the world. And our view of the world both the lights are sent horror files.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: We have a kind of a version of what life is. That is this trip from a maternity ward to the crematorium, or the cradle to the grave, and that there are certain things to be achieved.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: That society approves of, and that we feel very proud if we don't achieve them. And so on and so on. This is programs that are up to us.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And we think that this socially approved program, the social interpretation of the cosmic raw shot blood is what life is all about. What is nothing of the kind.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And the whole notion of Indian philosophy, Maksha, liberation, or the daerist philosophy, you see that the daerist sage is a person who doesn't buy the official Chinese cultural interpretation of what life is all about.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And therefore he may, or she may move after the mountains. And say, I've been fooled. I want to find out for myself without other people shouting at me all the time, what is really going on. What all this is.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So in the same way, in India, there is an institutionalized that when you've raised your family and can hand over the management to your son, you renounce your caste and become a son, yes, you abandon your identity and take a new name.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Instead of being Mr. Mukha Pajayya, you become Swami Kriya Nanda. You renounce your identity. And what you are supposed to be doing in the sense is saying now the game is over.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: The projection we have put on the raw shot blood has worn out. Let us find what lay behind it. Let us find what's really going on. Well, of course, when that gets institutionalized, there is an institutionalized version of the real world behind the socially conventionalized world.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: You have to watch out for that. Be very careful of that. When Swami is telling you that the world of Brahman is undifferentiated, all is one and give you the impression we were discussing this yesterday,

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: of that when you are awakened and have insight into everything being Brahman, all differentations will vanish.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And that's a conventional view of the universe in just the same measure as our ordinary everyday view of the universe's conventional. Don't get hooked on that.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So what I am talking about and the trouble is that I can't give you any authority for this. I can't say I represent some very authoritative group of people who really know what's what.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And that therefore I am giving you the message about what it really is and all these different social interpretations, cultural interpretations of the world of bunk, but there are those of us in the know who know what it's really all about, because that would just be another social convention.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So that's why I don't accept disciples or found the religion. But obviously there is a way in which you can see the world, for yourself.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: You may very well agree with what other people see and you'll be able to communicate that way of seeing two others.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It may be by no more than a glint in the eye whereby you will understand that somebody else sees it as the way you do.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So all our meditation exercises, all our practice is to simply open our consciousness to what is going on as distinct from what is said to be going on.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: So therefore we to do that we must suspend our words, we must suspend our descriptions and be alert to the actual happening.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: It's as simple as that because if you really get to the place where you don't talk about it, it's all perfectly clear.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: All the problems vanish, the problem of survival, the problem of even the problems of pain.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: If you're in the non-verbal dimension of consciousness, theology, philosophy, metaphysics, as we ordinary talk about them, absolutely cease to be urgent problems.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And you see the answer to all the questions, the theologians, and the metaphysicians asked. You see why their questions were absurd.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And you see exactly how it can be that this moment is what you were always looking for. The paradise, the far-off divine event which all creation moves.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: And it becomes absolutely clear. If you will, at least temporarily, suspend description, come on and experience life directly.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: You've been listening to the limits of language with Alan Watts.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: The limits of language has been restored and remastered and is available here on the being in the way podcast and also at play.allowawats.org, the Alan Watts streaming channel.

source: Alan Watts on the Limits of Language _ Being in the Way Podcast Ep. 28__watch
speaker: Ram Dass
text: Our theme music is by Zackir Hussein, courtesy of moment records, and Corey Leonard is our sound engineer. Thank you for tuning in.
